narrative:
Dear Student,

I understand that receiving a rejection for your university application can be disheartening, and I want to help you understand the reasons behind this decision. The model used to evaluate your application predicted a 50% probability that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt. This prediction led to the rejection of your application, as the university aims to admit students who are more likely to succeed in passing the bar exam on their first try.

Let's look at the factors that influenced this prediction. Your academic performance, particularly your grades in Year 1 and Year 3, were significant factors. You were placed in lower deciles compared to the average student, which means your grades were lower than most of your peers. This pushed the prediction towards a higher likelihood of failing the bar exam. Additionally, your LSAT score was below the average, which also contributed to the prediction of potential failure.

Your family income was another factor considered by the model. Being in a lower income bracket compared to the average student might have influenced the prediction, as financial constraints can sometimes impact academic performance. Your undergraduate GPA was also below average, which further contributed to the prediction of not passing the bar exam.

Other factors, such as your racial background and part-time status, had a smaller influence on the prediction. While these factors were considered, they were not as impactful as your academic performance and LSAT score. Your gender had the least influence on the prediction.

To improve your chances of passing the bar exam in the future, you might consider focusing on enhancing your academic performance and preparing thoroughly for the LSAT. Seeking additional academic support or resources could help you improve your grades and test scores. Remember, this prediction is not a definitive outcome but rather an assessment based on current data. With dedication and effort, you can work towards achieving your goal of passing the bar exam.
